name: ComposeLists
tagline: Render and manage collections in Compose: LazyColumn, LazyVerticalGrid, stable keys, multi-select, filtering, empty state.
whatItDoes: You see a 'Compose Lists' screen with a search field and a scrolling list of about twelve note cards, each showing a bold title and a few lines of body text. Typing in the search box instantly filters the notes (matched case-insensitively against both title and body); if nothing matches you get a 'No notes found' message that echoes your query. Tapping a card toggles its selection, highlighting it and showing a live 'N selected' count with a 'Clear selection' button. A toolbar button flips the same data between a one-column list (LazyColumn) and a two-column grid (LazyVerticalGrid). All of this state survives screen rotation because it lives in a ViewModel.
mentalModel: A list UI is a pure function of hoisted state: you keep the source list plus the query/selection/layout in one place, and the on-screen list is derived from them, with stable item keys ('key = { it.id }') tying each row to a note's identity rather than its position.

screens.name: NotesScreen
screens.role: Stateful root: obtains a NotesViewModel and forwards its query, notes, selection, and layout down to the stateless content.

screens.name: NotesContent
screens.role: Stateless UI shell: a Scaffold with the search field, the optional selection bar, and the switch between column and grid (or the empty state).

screens.name: NotesColumn
screens.role: Renders the notes as a single-column LazyColumn using stable keys.

screens.name: NotesGrid
screens.role: Renders the SAME notes as a two-column LazyVerticalGrid (GridCells.Fixed(2)) using the same stable keys.

screens.name: NoteCard
screens.role: One clickable note item (used by both layouts) whose background highlights when selected.

screens.name: EmptyState
screens.role: The fallback shown when filtering matches zero notes, echoing the failed query.

concepts.plain: Compose's scrolling list and grid that only compose the rows currently on screen. 'LazyColumn' stacks one column; 'LazyVerticalGrid' with 'GridCells.Fixed(2)' lays the same data in two columns. They replace RecyclerView with almost no boilerplate.

Passing 'key = { it.id }' to 'items(...)' tells Compose to track each row by the note's unique 'id' instead of its position. That preserves scroll position, selection highlight, and animations across filtering and reordering.

All mutable state (query, selectedIds, layout) lives in 'NotesViewModel', not inside the composables. The UI receives state as parameters and reports events via lambdas, so the UI is a predictable function of state.
'visibleNotes' is a computed property (a 'get()') that re-filters 'allNotes' by 'query' on every read. The filtered list is never stored, so there are no stale duplicates to keep in sync.
'toggleSelected' builds a brand-new 'Set<Int>' ('selectedIds - id' or '+ id') rather than mutating the old set. Replacing the reference is what lets Compose detect the change and recompose.

'NotesScreen' is the only composable that touches a ViewModel; 'NotesContent' and everything below it take state as parameters. This split is exactly what lets the '@Preview' functions render the UI with hand-supplied data and no ViewModel.

When 'notes.isEmpty()' the UI shows the dedicated 'EmptyState' composable instead of a blank screen, echoing the query so the user knows their search matched nothing rather than thinking the app broke.

'NotesViewModel' extends 'androidx.lifecycle.ViewModel' and is obtained via 'viewModel()'. Because the ViewModel outlives Activity recreation, the search text, selection, and layout are kept across rotation.

files.role: The entire sample: the Note data class and seed data, the NotesViewModel state holder, the stateful NotesScreen, the stateless NotesContent, the NotesColumn / NotesGrid lazy layouts, the NoteCard item, the EmptyState, and three @Preview functions.

Student-facing walkthrough of the list concepts, what to inspect, run instructions, and a historical note comparing LazyColumn/LazyVerticalGrid to the old RecyclerView + Adapter + ViewHolder approach.

highlights.title: The Note model and its stable id
highlights.explain: 'Note' is a plain immutable data class whose 'id' is the load-bearing field. That 'id' is the stable identity handed to 'key = { it.id }' in both lazy layouts, so it must be unique and never reused for a different note. 'title' and 'body' are both what the case-insensitive filter searches against.

highlights.title: The ViewModel holds query, selection, and layout
highlights.explain: 'NotesViewModel' keeps the private source list 'allNotes' plus three observable fields via 'by mutableStateOf' with 'private set': 'query', 'selectedIds', and 'layout'. Storing this in a ViewModel (not in the composables) is what makes it survive rotation and keeps the UI a pure function of state. Reading any of these inside a composable subscribes it to changes.

highlights.title: visibleNotes is DERIVED, not stored
highlights.explain: 'visibleNotes' is a 'get()' property that re-filters 'allNotes' by the trimmed 'query' every time it is read, matching with 'contains(q, ignoreCase = true)' against both title and body. Because the filtered list is recomputed rather than cached, there is no second copy to keep in sync. An empty query short-circuits to return everything.

highlights.title: Immutable selection toggle
highlights.explain: 'toggleSelected' assigns a NEW set ('selectedIds - id' to remove, or '+ id' to add) instead of mutating the existing one. Compose only notices a state change when the reference changes, so this immutability is precisely what triggers recomposition and updates the highlight and the selected-count.

highlights.title: Stateful screen forwards state to stateless content
highlights.explain: 'NotesScreen' is the only composable that obtains a ViewModel (via 'viewModel()' as a default parameter) and wires its state and method references down into 'NotesContent'. Note it passes 'viewModel.visibleNotes' (the derived, already-filtered list) and method refs like 'viewModel::toggleSelected'. Everything below this is a pure function of its parameters, which is why the previews need no ViewModel.

highlights.title: Stable keys in the LazyColumn
highlights.explain: The 'LazyColumn' calls 'items(notes, key = { it.id })', tying each composed row to a note's unique id rather than its index. When the list is filtered or reordered, Compose keeps and moves the correct rows instead of recomposing by position, preserving scroll and selection. 'NotesGrid' uses the identical pattern via the aliased 'gridItems', and 'note.id in selectedIds' is an O(1) membership test.

newVsPrev: ComposeModernUI taught single-screen Material 3 layout (Scaffold, TopAppBar, theming, individual composables). ComposeLists adds the COLLECTION dimension on top of that: it introduces LazyColumn and LazyVerticalGrid for efficiently rendering and switching layouts over a list, stable item keys ('key = { it.id }') for tracking item identity, multi-selection in a hoisted Set, text filtering via a derived 'visibleNotes' property, an explicit empty state, and a ViewModel (obtained with 'viewModel()') so list state survives rotation. The base project's Navigation 3 and kotlinx-serialization deps were removed because this sample is deliberately not about navigation.
tryThis: Delete the 'key = { it.id }' argument from the 'items(...)' call in NotesColumn, run the app, select a note near the bottom, then type a filter that removes notes above it. Watch the selection highlight jump to the wrong row, then add the key back to fix it.
Add a new Note to 'sampleNotes' (e.g. Note(13, 'Try this', 'My own note')) and confirm it appears in both the list and the grid, and that searching for 'try' surfaces it.
Change 'GridCells.Fixed(2)' in NotesGrid to 'GridCells.Fixed(3)' (or to 'GridCells.Adaptive(140.dp)') and observe how the grid reflows.

In 'visibleNotes', remove the 'note.body.contains(...)' half of the filter so it only matches titles, then search for a word that appears only in a body (e.g. 'squats') and watch that note disappear from results.

Rotate the device/emulator while notes are selected and the search box has text; confirm the state is retained, then move 'query' into a 'remember { mutableStateOf("") }' inside a composable to see it get lost on rotation instead.
gotchas: The layout toggle is a text-only TextButton whose label shows what you will switch TO ('Grid' while in column, 'List' while in grid). It deliberately avoids a material-icons dependency, so there is no icon, just the word.

'visibleNotes' is a recomputed 'get()' property, so it filters the whole list on every read. That is fine for a dozen hard-coded notes but would be wasteful at scale; a real app would use 'derivedStateOf' or expose the filtered list as a 'StateFlow'.

Selection is keyed on 'Note.id' (a Set<Int>), not on the Note object or its position. This is why stable keys matter and why ids must be unique and stable; reusing an id for a different note would break both selection and item identity.

There is exactly one screen and no navigation here by design. The seed data is hard-coded in 'sampleNotes' rather than coming from a database or network, so editing/adding notes at runtime is not supported.
runTest: Build and install from the project root with './gradlew assembleDebug' then './gradlew installDebug' (or open in Android Studio and press Run). On screen: type in the search box and confirm the list filters live against both title and body; search 'xyz' to confirm the 'No notes found' empty state that echoes your query; tap several cards and confirm each highlights with a live 'N selected' count and that 'Clear selection' resets them; tap the toolbar 'Grid'/'List' button and confirm the SAME notes redraw as a two-column grid versus a single column; rotate the device and confirm the query, selection, and layout are all retained (proving the ViewModel). There are no deep links in this project.
